reason: Both competed for major commissions in Florence and Rome; their contrasting styles and temperaments made them the defining rivals of Renaissance art.

Lorenzo de' Medici recognized Leonardo's talent early, included him in his Florentine court, and launched his career by sending him to Milan as part of a diplomatic mission to Ludovico Sforza around 1482.
Leonardo served as Cesare Borgia's chief military architect and engineer in 1502-1503, traveling with his forces, mapping territories in central Italy, and designing fortifications.

Francis I invited Leonardo to France in 1516, provided him with Chateau du Clos Luce near Amboise and a royal pension, and reportedly held him as he died in 1519 -- the most generous patron of Leonardo's final years.

Lorenzo de' Medici invited the teenage Michelangelo to live in his household around 1489, treating him as a member of the family and exposing him to the leading humanists of Florence -- the formative patronage of his early career.

Bramante reportedly persuaded Julius II to assign Michelangelo the Sistine Chapel ceiling, hoping the unfamiliar fresco medium would embarrass him; Michelangelo later accused Bramante of sabotage and sustained personal enmity.

Julius II commissioned the Sistine Chapel ceiling and Michelangelo's own tomb, driving one of the most productive and stormy patron-artist relationships in history -- Michelangelo once fled Rome to escape the pope's demands.

Clement VII commissioned the Medici Chapel and the Laurentian Library from Michelangelo, two defining works of the High Renaissance; the relationship persisted through the Sack of Rome despite Michelangelo briefly taking up arms against Clement's allies.

Paul III commissioned the Last Judgment (1536-1541) and appointed Michelangelo chief architect of St. Peter's Basilica, making him the dominant artistic force in Rome for the final three decades of his career.

Pope Leo X issued the bull Exsurge Domine (1520) threatening excommunication and then formally excommunicated Luther in January 1521, making their confrontation the opening act of the Protestant Reformation.

Initially sympathetic to Luther's criticisms of the Church, Erasmus broke with him over free will: his De libero arbitrio (1524) directly attacked Luther, who responded with De servo arbitrio (1525) in one of the era's defining theological debates.
Charles V convened the Diet of Worms (1521) where Luther famously refused to recant, then issued the Edict of Worms declaring Luther a heretic and outlaw of the Empire.

Both Protestant reformers, Luther and Zwingli clashed irreconcilably at the Marburg Colloquy (1529) over the nature of the Eucharist, splitting the Reformation movement into Lutheran and Reformed branches.
